s: Built for 500 enslaved people; became the Supreme Court
w: The VOC built this in 1679 to house enslaved workers from East Africa, Madagascar, India and Southeast Asia -- up to 500 people in these walls. After emancipation it became government offices, then the Old Supreme Court, then a cultural museum. Renamed the Slave Lodge in 1998: the first time the word appeared on the door.
_facts: Built 1679 by the Dutch East India Company (VOC)
Housed up to 500 enslaved people from East Africa, Madagascar, India and Southeast Asia
Became government offices 1811, then Old Supreme Court 1911-1960s
Opened as South African Cultural History Museum in 1967
Renamed Slave Lodge in 1998 under Iziko Museums of South Africa

s: Mandela spoke from this balcony hours after walking free
w: On 11 February 1990, hours after his release from Victor Verster Prison, Nelson Mandela stood on this honey-limestone balcony and gave his first public speech in 27 years to the Grand Parade crowd below. The Edwardian building opened in 1905; its stone came from Bath, England; its organ has 3,165 pipes. A Mandela statue now marks the spot. The crowd was there first.
_facts: Completed 1900, formally opened 1905, Edwardian style
Built from oolitic limestone imported from Bath, England
Nelson Mandela gave first public speech from the balcony 11 February 1990 after 27 years imprisoned
Organ by Norman and Beard of London with 3,165 pipes
Mandela statue unveiled on the balcony July 2018
Filming location for The Crown and Black Sails

n: Zeitz Museum of Contemporary Art Africa
s: Grain silos carved into a cathedral for African art
w: Between 1921 and 1924, workers stacked 42 concrete cylinders 57 metres high at the harbour mouth to store grain. When Thomas Heatherwick's studio got the commission, they cut directly into the cylinders with diamond blades, hollowing a cathedral atrium from the packed concrete. Nine floors of galleries make it the largest museum of contemporary African art in the world. The grain is gone; the scale is not.
_facts: Grain silos built 1921-1924 at V&A Waterfront
Thomas Heatherwick Studio carved 42 concrete cylinders to create galleries and central atrium
Opened 22 September 2017
9,500 sq m total space across nine floors; 6,000 sq m exhibition space
Largest museum of contemporary art from Africa and its diaspora
RIBA International List 2018

s: The oldest house in Bo-Kaap, dressed as 1800
w: Jan de Waal built rental cottages on Signal Hill's slopes from 1763; this one is the last in its original form. Iziko has dressed the rooms as a prosperous 19th-century Cape Muslim household -- bridal chamber, drop-leaf dining table, Regency chairs. The voorstoep out front, with a bench at each end, was how neighbours talked across the theological and social distances the street always held.
_facts: Built by Jan de Waal from 1763; oldest house in Bo-Kaap still in original form
Displays 19th-century Cape Muslim domestic life including bridal chamber and Cape Regency furniture
Features traditional voorstoep (front terrace with benches at each end)
Operated by Iziko Museums of South Africa
Bo-Kaap is the historical centre of Cape Malay culture on the slopes of Signal Hill

s: Slave market turned flea market turned protest stage
w: Cape Town's oldest public square -- a slave and vegetable market from 1696 -- found its moment on 2 September 1989. Police fired a water cannon loaded with purple dye at anti-apartheid marchers; a protester seized the nozzle and turned it on the National Party offices. From that day, 'The purple shall govern' became one of the great lines of the resistance. The Dutch Masters hang in the 1761 Old Town House at the edge of the cobbles.
_facts: Established 1696 with a burgher watch house
Functioned as a slave market and vegetable market historically
Old Town House (1761) holds the Michaelis Collection of Dutch Masters
Purple Rain Protest: 2 September 1989 -- police used purple dye water cannon on Mass Democratic Movement marchers
Protester Philip Ivey redirected the cannon nozzle at National Party offices
Slogan 'The purple shall govern' emerged from the protest

s: Africa's first museum, with a whale suspended through the floors
w: Founded in 1825 -- South Africa's first museum -- and moved to this neoclassical building in the Company's Garden in 1897. The centrepiece is the Whale Well: a 20.5-metre blue whale skeleton hung through four Victorian floors, visible from galleries above and below. Elsewhere, the Bushman Dioramas -- body casts taken between 1907 and 1924 -- now carry a long and necessary explanatory frame.
_facts: Founded 1825, first museum in South Africa
Moved to current location in Company's Garden in 1897
Contains a 20.5-metre blue whale skeleton in the multi-storey 'Whale Well'
Bushman Diorama contains body casts from a casting project 1907-1924
Over 1.5 million scientific specimens including 250-million-year-old Karoo fossils
Planetarium on site

s: Oldest synagogue in Southern Africa is its front door
w: The 1863 Old Shul -- the oldest synagogue in Southern Africa -- is the entrance. Beyond it, the museum traces South African Jewish history from Lithuanian origins to apartheid-era activism: Jewish lawyers and jurists were central to the anti-apartheid legal defence. Nelson Mandela, with Helen Suzman at his side, opened the museum in 2000. The connection between that 1863 congregation and the country they helped build is the whole argument.
_facts: Old Shul (1863) at the entrance is the oldest synagogue in Southern Africa
Founded by philanthropist Mendel Kaplan
Opened by President Nelson Mandela in 2000, with Helen Suzman present
Documents South African Jewish life, with roots largely in Lithuania
Located in Gardens, near the national gallery and parliament

s: Africa's first Holocaust centre; the parallel to apartheid is explicit
w: Opened in 1999, five years after the end of apartheid, Africa's first Holocaust museum does not sidestep the comparison: the exhibitions draw a line between Nuremberg Race Laws and the Group Areas Act, between ghetto and township. More than 5,000 South African teachers have trained here. The building adjoins the oldest synagogue in Southern Africa.
_facts: Opened 1999 as Africa's first Holocaust centre
Part of the South African Holocaust & Genocide Foundation (SAHGF) alongside centres in Johannesburg and Durban
Only accredited Holocaust education training provider in South Africa
Has trained over 5,000 teachers
Adjacent to the South African Jewish Museum and Old Shul (1863)

s: Cape Town's only surviving Georgian red-brick house
w: John Barker -- a Yorkshire attorney who arrived at the Cape in 1823 -- spent fifteen years building this house and named it for his dead wife, Ann Bertram Findlay. Red brick, two storeys, 1839-1854: the only unpainted Georgian house of its kind left in the city. Restored in the 1980s with Welsh slate and original paint. The Anne Lidderdale Collection of Georgian furniture and Chinese porcelain is inside, as quiet as the name on the wall.
_facts: Built 1839-1854 by John Barker, Yorkshire attorney who emigrated to Cape Colony in 1823
Named for deceased wife Ann Bertram Findlay
Only surviving unpainted red brick two-storey Georgian house in Cape Town
Declared a national monument in 1962
Restored 1983-84 including Welsh slate for the roof
Houses the Anne Lidderdale Collection of Georgian furniture, English and Chinese porcelain

s: A VOC gun battery buried under the harbour for 200 years
w: The VOC built this U-shaped battery between 1714 and 1725 -- 16 guns trained on the harbour mouth -- and named it for governor Maurits Pasques de Chavonnes. It was decommissioned, built over, and forgotten. In the 1990s, construction crews expanding the Clock Tower Precinct broke through and found the walls, a well, and the battery's footprint intact. UCT archaeologists dug it out. An office building now sits above it; the original stonework is in the basement.
_facts: Built 1714-1725 by the Dutch East India Company (VOC)
Named for Cape governor Maurits Pasques de Chavonnes
U-shaped fortification with 16 guns protecting the harbour
Buried under expanding harbour works and forgotten
Rediscovered by UCT archaeologists during V&A Waterfront development in the 1990s
Now a museum with original walls visible below ground level

s: Italian embassy ballroom; Cape Town's arthouse cinema since the 70s
w: Princess Labia opened this as an Italian legation ballroom in May 1949. When the Artscape Theatre pulled audiences away, a Polish emigrant let a film programmer use the space for arthouse and foreign-language films, and the place found its identity. Ludi Kraus has run it since 1989; the original ticket booth, sweets counter and seats survived. The crowd-funded digital projector now sits in a room that once hosted diplomatic receptions.
_facts: Opened 16 May 1949 as Italian Embassy ballroom by Princess Labia
Converted to arthouse cinema in early 1970s when Wolf Miknowski allowed Eric Liknaitzky to screen independent films
Ludi Kraus has owned the cinema since 1989
Original ticket booth, sweets counter and seats preserved
2014 crowdfunding campaign raised over 550,000 Rand from 885 supporters for digital projectors

s: Cape Town's oldest black township -- named 'sun', born of apartheid law
w: South Africa's 1923 Urban Areas Act mandated segregated Black suburbs. Langa -- 'sun' in Xhosa -- was Cape Town's first, opened in 1927. On 21 March 1960, the same day as the Sharpeville massacre, marchers left Langa to protest pass laws in the city; police opened fire here too. The township remained a centre of organised resistance for decades after. Tours run from within the community.
_facts: Formally opened 1927; Cape Town's oldest designated Black township
Name means 'sun' in Xhosa
Established under the Urban Areas Act of 1923
Major centre of anti-apartheid resistance
Anti-pass-law protests launched from Langa on 21 March 1960, same day as Sharpeville massacre

s: National collection since 1872; Kentridge's apartheid films are here
w: South Africa's national art collection dates from 1872; the neoclassical building on Government Avenue was not finished until 1930. The first fine art exhibition ever held in the country was staged here, in a predecessor building, in 1851. William Kentridge's animated film cycles about apartheid-era Johannesburg -- among the most significant works made in the country -- are in the permanent collection. Dutch and Flemish masters share the wall space with Zulu beadwork.
_facts: National collection founded 1872
Current neoclassical building constructed 1914-1930, inaugurated by Earl of Athlone
First fine art exhibition in South Africa held on this site in 1851
Holdings include Dutch, French and British 17th-19th century works and authoritative collection of South African sculpture and beadwork
Permanent collection includes William Kentridge animated film series (1989-1996)
Part of Iziko Museums since 2001
